name: Concurrency and Parallelism in Python
description: Master concurrent and parallel programming in Python using threading for I/O-bound tasks, multiprocessing for CPU-bound tasks, asyncio for asynchronous I/O, and concurrent.futures for high-level parallelism.

lessons.title: Threading
lessons.description: Threading enables concurrent execution of I/O-bound tasks by running multiple threads within a single process, though limited by the Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) for CPU-bound operations.
lessons.example:
import threading
import time
import queue
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

# Basic thread
def worker(name, delay):
    print(f"Thread {name} starting")
    time.sleep(delay)
    print(f"Thread {name} finished after {delay}s")

threads = []
for i in range(3):
    t = threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(f"Worker-{i}", i+1))
    t.start()
    threads.append(t)

for t in threads:
    t.join()
print("All threads completed")

# Thread with shared data and locks
counter = 0
lock = threading.Lock()

def increment():
    global counter
    for _ in range(100000):
        with lock:
            counter += 1

threads = []
for _ in range(5):
    t = threading.Thread(target=increment)
    t.start()
    threads.append(t)

for t in threads:
    t.join()
print(f"Counter value: {counter}")  # Should be 500000

# Producer-consumer pattern with Queue
def producer(queue, items):
    for item in items:
        print(f"Producing: {item}")
        queue.put(item)
        time.sleep(0.1)
    queue.put(None)  # Sentinel

def consumer(queue):
    while True:
        item = queue.get()
        if item is None:
            break
        print(f"Consuming: {item}")
        time.sleep(0.2)

q = queue.Queue()
prod = threading.Thread(target=producer, args=(q, range(10)))
cons = threading.Thread(target=consumer, args=(q,))

prod.start()
cons.start()
prod.join()
cons.join()

# ThreadPoolExecutor
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, as_completed

def process_url(url):
    time.sleep(1)
    return f"Processed {url}"

urls = [f"http://example.com/{i}" for i in range(10)]

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) as executor:
    futures = [executor.submit(process_url, url) for url in urls]
    for future in as_completed(futures):
        print(future.result())

# Thread-local data
thread_local = threading.local()

def set_and_get(value):
    thread_local.data = value
    time.sleep(0.1)
    return thread_local.data

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=3) as executor:
    results = list(executor.map(set_and_get, [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]))
print(f"Thread-local results: {results}")

# Event for synchronization
import random

def waiter(event):
    print("Waiting for event...")
    event.wait()
    print("Event received!")

def setter(event):
    time.sleep(random.uniform(0.5, 2))
    event.set()
    print("Event set!")

event = threading.Event()
threading.Thread(target=waiter, args=(event,)).start()
threading.Thread(target=setter, args=(event,)).start()

lessons.title: Multiprocessing
lessons.description: Multiprocessing enables true parallelism by spawning separate processes, bypassing the GIL for CPU-bound tasks with isolated memory spaces.

lessons.title: Asyncio
lessons.description: Asyncio provides asynchronous I/O using coroutines and an event loop, enabling high concurrency for network and I/O-bound applications.
lessons.example:
import asyncio
import aiohttp
import time

# Basic coroutine
async def say_hello(delay, name):
    await asyncio.sleep(delay)
    print(f"Hello {name}! (after {delay}s)")
    return f"{name} finished"

async def main_basic():
    # Run coroutines concurrently
    results = await asyncio.gather(
        say_hello(2, "Alice"),
        say_hello(1, "Bob"),
        say_hello(3, "Charlie")
    )
    print(f"All done: {results}")

# Run async function
asyncio.run(main_basic())

# Task management
async def worker(name, queue):
    while True:
        item = await queue.get()
        if item is None:
            break
        print(f"Worker {name} processing item {item}")
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
        queue.task_done()
    print(f"Worker {name} done")

async def main_worker():
    queue = asyncio.Queue()
    
    # Add items
    for i in range(10):
        await queue.put(i)
    
    # Start workers
    workers = [asyncio.create_task(worker(f"W{i}", queue)) for i in range(3)]
    
    # Wait for queue to be processed
    await queue.join()
    
    # Stop workers
    for _ in workers:
        await queue.put(None)
    
    await asyncio.gather(*workers)

asyncio.run(main_worker())

# Async HTTP requests
async def fetch_url(session, url):
    try:
        async with session.get(url) as response:
            return await response.text()
    except Exception as e:
        return f"Error: {e}"

async def fetch_multiple_urls(urls):
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [fetch_url(session, url) for url in urls]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
        return results

# Example usage
urls = [
    "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/1",
    "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/2",
    "https://jsonplaceholder.typicode.com/posts/3"
]
# results = asyncio.run(fetch_multiple_urls(urls))
# print(f"Fetched {len(results)} URLs")

# Async context managers
async def managed_resource():
    print("Acquiring resource")
    await asyncio.sleep(0.5)
    try:
        yield "resource"
    finally:
        print("Releasing resource")
        await asyncio.sleep(0.5)

async def use_resource():
    async with managed_resource() as resource:
        print(f"Using {resource}")
        await asyncio.sleep(1)

asyncio.run(use_resource())

# Async generators
async def async_range(stop):
    for i in range(stop):
        await asyncio.sleep(0.1)
        yield i

async def process_async_range():
    async for i in async_range(5):
        print(f"Processing {i}")

asyncio.run(process_async_range())

# Asyncio timeout
def long_operation():
    time.sleep(2)
    return "Result"

async def main_timeout():
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    try:
        result = await asyncio.wait_for(
            loop.run_in_executor(None, long_operation),
            timeout=1.0
        )
        print(f"Result: {result}")
    except asyncio.TimeoutError:
        print("Operation timed out")

asyncio.run(main_timeout())

# Asyncio semaphore for rate limiting
async def limited_fetch(session, url, semaphore):
    async with semaphore:
        return await fetch_url(session, url)

async def main_rate_limited():
    semaphore = asyncio.Semaphore(3)
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        tasks = [limited_fetch(session, url, semaphore) for url in urls]
        results = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
        return results

# asyncio.run(main_rate_limited())

lessons.title: Concurrent Futures
lessons.description: Concurrent.futures provides a high-level interface for asynchronously executing callables using threads or processes.
lessons.example:
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor, ProcessPoolExecutor, as_completed, wait
import time
import math
import random

# Common task functions
def cpu_intensive(n):
    """CPU-bound task: calculate sum of squares"""
    total = 0
    for i in range(n):
        total += i * i
    return total

def io_intensive(delay):
    """I/O-bound task: simulate network call"""
    time.sleep(delay)
    return f"Completed after {delay}s"

# ThreadPoolExecutor for I/O tasks
print("ThreadPoolExecutor for I/O tasks:")
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) as executor:
    delays = [1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 1, 2]
    results = list(executor.map(io_intensive, delays))
    print(f"Results: {results[:5]}...")  # Show first 5

# ProcessPoolExecutor for CPU tasks
print("\nProcessPoolExecutor for CPU tasks:")
with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=4) as executor:
    numbers = [1000000, 2000000, 3000000, 4000000]
    results = list(executor.map(cpu_intensive, numbers))
    print(f"Results: {results}")

# Submitting tasks and processing as they complete
print("\nProcessing tasks as they complete:")
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=3) as executor:
    futures = [executor.submit(io_intensive, random.uniform(0.5, 2)) for _ in range(10)]
    
    for future in as_completed(futures):
        result = future.result()
        print(f"Completed: {result}")

# Wait for all tasks to complete
print("\nWaiting for all tasks:")
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) as executor:
    futures = [executor.submit(io_intensive, 2) for _ in range(5)]
    done, not_done = wait(futures, timeout=3)
    print(f"Completed: {len(done)}, Pending: {len(not_done)}")

# Error handling in futures
def error_prone_task(x):
    if x < 0:
        raise ValueError("Negative value not allowed")
    return math.sqrt(x)

print("\nError handling:")
with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) as executor:
    futures = [executor.submit(error_prone_task, x) for x in [16, -4, 25, -9, 36]]
    
    for future in as_completed(futures):
        try:
            result = future.result()
            print(f"Success: {result}")
        except Exception as e:
            print(f"Error: {e}")

# Callback with future
print("\nFuture callbacks:")
def callback(future):
    print(f"Callback: {future.result()}")

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) as executor:
    future = executor.submit(io_intensive, 1)
    future.add_done_callback(callback)
    time.sleep(1.5)

# Chaining futures
def multiply(x):
    return x * 2

def add_one(x):
    return x + 1

def process_pipeline():
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) as executor:
        future = executor.submit(multiply, 10)
        future = executor.submit(add_one, future.result())
        return future.result()

print(f"\nPipeline result: {process_pipeline()}")

# Mixing ThreadPool and ProcessPool
def hybrid_task(data):
    # CPU part (executed in process pool)
    processed = cpu_intensive(data)
    # I/O part would be in thread pool
    return processed

print("\nHybrid approach:")
with ProcessPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) as process_executor:
    cpu_futures = [process_executor.submit(cpu_intensive, 1000000) for _ in range(4)]
    
    with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=2) as thread_executor:
        io_futures = [thread_executor.submit(io_intensive, 1) for _ in range(4)]
        
        cpu_results = [f.result() for f in cpu_futures]
        io_results = [f.result() for f in io_futures]
        
        print(f"CPU results: {cpu_results}")
        print(f"IO results: {io_results}")

# Canceling futures
print("\nCanceling futures:")
def long_running():
    time.sleep(10)
    return "Never reached"

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1) as executor:
    future = executor.submit(long_running)
    time.sleep(0.5)
    future.cancel()
    print(f"Cancelled: {future.cancelled()}")
    print(f"Done: {future.done()}")

# Maximum workers calculation
import os

cpu_count = os.cpu_count()
print(f"\nSystem has {cpu_count} CPU cores")
print(f"Optimal thread pool size for I/O: {cpu_count * 4}")
print(f"Optimal process pool size for CPU: {cpu_count}")
